Jeremy Cohen

  • posts

Mapping the Human Taste Palate with Matrix Factorization

January 22, 2015

Summary: My classmates and I applied the matrix factorization technique for recommender systems to a data set of 120K user profiles scraped from the recipe-sharing web site AllRecipes and extracted 40 “latent tastes” that combine in various proportions to make up all sorts of human foods.

If you understood that sentence, feel free to skip to part II of this post, where you can view the results and play around with some visualizations.

On the other hand, if you want to learn about matrix factorization in order to pique your intellectual curiosity, impress someone at a job interview, or pick up people at bars, read on.

A Gentle Introduction to Matrix Factorization

First off, I’m sorry.

I know that the term “matrix factorization” is liable to trigger a life-threatening reaction in persons allergic to linear algebra. If reading the title of this blog post is causing to you go into anaphylactic shock, please know that I meant no harm.

The truth is that I didn’t pay any attention in linear algebra class either. As far as I can tell, “orthogonal” is just a fancy synonym for “unrelated” that I should use when I want to sound smart, an “eigenvector” is probably something horrible that Werner von Braun dreamed up before he switched employers in 1945, and a “product space” sounds like something that an undergrad business major living in SoMa would get excited about.

The good news is that you don’t need to know any more linear algebra than Keanu Reeves in order to understand matrix factorization.

Before I get into the details, I want to motivate the algorithm by pointing out an application to the most heavily studied problem in computer science: how to get people to buy more things.

Techniques for Recommender Systems

Suppose that I order the City Chic First Years Jet Stroller on Amazon.com. Amazon would probably like to sell me more items, so that it can make more money, with which it can continue to pay its software engineers poorly. So what items should Amazon recommend?

One idea is to recommend me items that are intrinsically similar to the City Chic First Years Jet Stroller. These might be items that also have “stroller” in the name, or items that are made by the same manufacturer, or items whose descriptions share words in common with the description of the City Chic First Years Jet Stroller. This is called content-based recommendation.

A more interesting idea is to treat the items as black boxes and somehow learn correlations between the items from the purchasing history of all users. This is called collaborative filtering. For example, people who buy baby strollers are probably also likely to buy, say, the Essential Mozart CD collection. Now, maybe someday we’ll have #deeplearning algorithms that can learn facts about the universe (in this example, that American parents are crazy) on their own, just from watching Vines of cats or whatever. Until that day, we’ll have to settle for matrix factorization.

Matrix Factorization: The Intuition

As an insufferable ninth grader, I took a bunch of online quizzes during the 2008 U.S. presidential election cycle. These quizzes would ask a zillion questions about my personal political opinions, and then they would show me a Cartesian plane with two dimensions—social views and economic views—with a big dot somewhere that was supposed to represent me. There were also dots for Barack Obama and John McCain, as well as for other notable figures like Karl Marx, Ayn Rand, Margaret Thatcher, and Abraham Lincoln, who had all apparently taken the quiz. Everyone could be reduced to two numbers: their score on the “social views” scale, and their score on the “economic views” scale.

This is basically how the matrix factorization technique for recommender systems works.

Matrix factorization and dubious ideology diagrams are both forms of dimensionality reduction. Political platforms probably have hundreds of dimensions, like taxation, immigration, energy, national security, and climate change. But the ideology quiz reduces every politician to just two: “social” and “economic.” This is neat because estimating my affinity for a candidate no longer requires knowing his or her opinion on the Keystone XL pipeline Then, to estimate my affinity for a candidate, I don’t need to know his or her opinion on the Keystone XL pipeline or on the ; I just need to know their coordinates on social and economic axes.

In a similar way, matrix factorization reduces the dimensionality of the space of item properties down to a small number of factors. Every item is represented by a vector of weights quantifying the extent to which each factor is present in the item, and every user is represented by a vector of weights quantifying the user’s preference for each factor.

Imagine a big U x I matrix where each row corresponds to a user, and each column corresponds to an item. The entry in the (u, i) square is the rating that user u gives to item i. If user u didn’t rate item i, then the (u, i) square is empty. Indeed, most squares are empty. The goal of a recommender system is to fill in the empty squares given the observed ones.

Matrix factorization assumes that all items can be represented by K hidden factors, and that the U x I user-item matrix is really the product of a U x K user-factor matrix and a K x I factor-item matrix.

The (u, k) entry in the user-factor matrix quantifies user u’s affinity for factor k. The (k, i) entry in the factor-item matrix quantifies the extent to which factor k is present in item i.

use the word orthogonal

Matrix Factorization and AllRecipes

I took a machine learning course with an open-ended final project. The question my group asked ourselves is:

Can we use matrix factorization to discover a set of basic human tastes in food?

We decided to get our data from AllRecipes.com, a popular recipe sharing web site. With tens of thousands of recipes and over a hundred thousand users, AllRecipes was a food data bonanza.

Each AllRecipes user has a “recipe box” where he or she can list his or her favorite recipes. It was these “recipe boxes” that we scraped. After discarding all recipes that occurred in fewer than 300 recipe boxes, we were left with 9,546 recipes and 115,308 users.

We used the NMF MATLAB toolbox to run non-negative matrix factorization on the resulting 9,546 × 115,308 matrix. The algorithm took 8 hours to complete 2,000 iterations on a machine with a 2.2GHz processor and 128 GB RAM.

[Image here.]

The next morning, we checked the results and arrived at our answer to the question above:

Yes! (Well, sort of!)

Sure enough, to our amusement (and mild surprise) the factors produced by non-negative matrix factorization really do seem to correspond to human tastes! In this post, I will explore three different ways to play around with the data generated by the factorization:

First, we can ask questions like: “What does factor #3 really represent?” The easiest way to find out is to look at the dishes with the highest coefficients in factor #3. The top ones are “Quinoa and Black Beans,” “Baked Kale Chips,” and “Quinoa Side Dish.” We can conclude that factor #3 represents vegan food.

Second, we can ask questions like: “Which factors does ‘Maple Salmon’ consist of, and in what proportions?” Answering this question is as simple as retrieving the row for “Maple Salmon” from the factors-by-items matrix produced by NMF.

Use the Taste Breakdown visualization tool below to break down any recipe into its consituent factors.

Third, we can ask questions like “What food would we get if we smashed together the ‘vegetarian’ factor with the ‘cookies/brownies’ factor?”

Look at the 40 individual factors and see how 2. For any recipe, can view its breakdown into factors. 3.

The 40 factors are displayed below. For each factor, the five recipes with the highest coeffecients in the factor are shown.

comments

Tastes

Show all 40 tastes.

A few highlights:

  • Some tastes correspond to food groups, like seafood (taste 4), bread (taste 24), salad (taste 12), and beverages, both alcoholic (taste 22) and non-alcoholic (taste 23).

  • Other tastes correspond to dietary preferences, like vegan (tastes 3, 10) and barbecue (taste 31).

  • Still others correspond to national holidays, like Thanksgiving (taste 32) and Superbowl (taste 7).

  • In a sure sign that AllRecipes users have their priorities straight, a large plurality of tastes are used to differentiate between subtly different kinds of desserts, such as cake (taste 8), cheesecake (taste 19), fruit pastries (tastes 16, 18, 25), cookies (taste 35), confections (taste 38), scones / biscotti (taste 40), and fancifully nutritious desserts (taste 11).

  • Taste 6 is entirely devoted to slow-cooker foods, as taste 39 is to casserole. I fear that AllRecipes.com may be one place, along with the Social Security budget committee, where the nation’s grandmothers hold too much influence.

  • Taste 28 corresponds to Indian / Thai food. I was faintly surprised that no other ethnic cuisines came out of the factorization, although I suppose taste 39 (casserole) might qualify as Mexican, and taste 27 has a definite German flair.

  • Taste 13 comprises dishes—macaroni and cheese, meat loaf, lasagna, potato salad—that can only be described as “comfort food.” This took me by surprise. I had always assumed that the concept of “comfort food” as a distinct class of foods was a mere figment of Paula Deen’s imagination, sort of like the concept of “bacon biscuits deep fried in butter that don’t give you diabetes.” Apparently, I was mistaken.

  • Most of the remaining tastes correspond to chicken in various states of matter.

Taste Breakdown

Here is a visualization tool that shows the breakdown of each dish into the top three constituent factors:

permalink

Here are some examples of obvious facts that have been confirmed using 8 hours of CPU time:

  • Naan is a combination of Indian food (taste 28) and bread (taste 24).

  • Blueberry Waffles with Fast Blueberry Sauce is mix of fruit desserts (taste 25), breakfast foods (taste 34), and granola (taste 11).

  • Butter Chickpea Curry is a cross between vegetarian food (taste 10), and Indian food (taste 28).

  • Cedar Planked Salmon is part seafood (taste 4) and part barbecue (taste 31).

  • Coq Au Vin is popular among people who also like both alcoholic beverages (taste 22) and chicken (taste 14).

Mix ‘n Match

Click on some tastes to begin!

permalink

Dishes closely matching the selected taste profile:

For a project in a machine learning course, my group decided to see if we could use matrix factorization on a domain near and dear to all of us

This post describes a project carried out for David Blei’s course “COS 424: Interacting with Data” in Spring 2014 at Princeton University by Rob Sami (Google), Aaron Schild (Berkeley PhD), Spencer Tank (Palantir), and me.

Thanks to Richard Nixon for reviewing and revising the draft.

Discussion, links, and tweets

I'm a developer at GitHub. Follow me on Twitter; you'll enjoy my tweets. I take care to carefully craft each one. Or at least aim to make you giggle. Or offended. One of those two— I haven't decided which yet.
